# Scrum Master / Team Lead Survey — TimeHuddle Market Research

> Copy these questions into a Google Form. Each `---` is a new section break.

**Form title**: How Do You Plan and Run Your Team's Ceremonies?
**Form description**: We're building a tool for scrum masters and team leads and
want to understand how you actually plan sprints, run standups, and track team
capacity. This takes about 7–10 minutes. No right or wrong answers — we want
honest impressions of what's working and what isn't.

---

## Section 1: About You

**Q1 — What's your primary role?**
*Multiple choice*
- Scrum master
- Engineering manager
- Tech lead / team lead
- Product manager
- Project manager
- Other: ___

**Q2 — How many people are on the team(s) you manage or facilitate?**
*Multiple choice*
- 2–5
- 6–10
- 11–20
- More than 20

**Q3 — Do you follow an agile methodology?**
*Multiple choice*
- Yes — Scrum
- Yes — Kanban
- Yes — a hybrid / our own version
- Not formally, but we do standups and planning
- No

---

## Section 2: Standups

**Q4 — How do you run your daily standups?**
*Multiple choice — pick all that apply*
- Video call (Zoom, Meet, Teams)
- In-person
- Async (Slack bot, written update)
- Dedicated standup tool
- Other: ___

**Q5 — What do you actually do during a standup? Walk us through it.**
*Long text — open ended*

**Q6 — What's the most painful or wasteful part of running standups right now?**
*Long text — open ended*

**Q7 — How do you track blockers that come up in standups?**
*Multiple choice — pick all that apply*
- Written in a shared doc or Confluence
- Created as a ticket in Jira/Linear/etc.
- Slack message / channel pin
- Verbally — rely on people to remember
- We don't have a consistent system
- Other: ___

**Q8 — How often do standups run long? What causes it?**
*Long text — open ended*

---

## Section 3: Sprint Planning and Capacity

**Q9 — How do you currently figure out how much work your team can take on in a
sprint?**
*Long text — open ended*

**Q10 — Do you track team member availability (time off, partial days, recurring
meetings) when planning a sprint?**
*Multiple choice*
- Yes — we have a formal system for this
- Somewhat — I ask people informally
- Not really — I mostly estimate from experience
- No — we don't account for it

**Q11 — Has your team over-committed to a sprint because of untracked time off
or other unavailability?**
*Multiple choice*
- Yes, frequently
- Yes, occasionally
- Rarely
- Never

**Q12 — If you could see each team member's available hours for the sprint
before planning — accounting for time off and recurring meetings — how much
would that change how you plan?**
*Scale: 1 (wouldn't change anything) → 5 (would fundamentally change how I plan)*

**Q13 — What tools do you use today to track team capacity or availability?**
*Long text — open ended (e.g. Harvest Forecast, spreadsheet, nothing)*

---

## Section 4: Visibility and Reporting

**Q14 — After a sprint ends, how do you know how much time was actually spent on
what?**
*Long text — open ended*

**Q15 — Do you produce any kind of report for stakeholders about what your team
worked on?**
*Multiple choice*
- Yes, regularly (weekly or per sprint)
- Sometimes, when asked
- Rarely
- No

**Q16 — If you produce reports, how long does it take to pull the data together?**
*Multiple choice*
- Under 15 minutes
- 15–60 minutes
- 1–3 hours
- More than 3 hours
- N/A — I don't produce reports

---

## Section 5: AI and Automation

**Q17 — Before sprint planning, if an AI summarized each team member's recent
work and suggested a capacity-appropriate story point target based on historical
velocity — would you use it?**
*Multiple choice*
- Yes, absolutely
- Probably yes
- Not sure
- Probably not
- No

**Q18 — What would you be most skeptical about with AI involvement in sprint
planning or standup facilitation?**
*Long text — open ended*

---

## Section 6: Anything Else

**Q19 — What tool or feature would make the biggest difference to how you run
your team ceremonies?**
*Long text — open ended*

**Q20 — Any other thoughts you'd like to share?**
*Long text — optional*
